Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd usually try to set aside some hours on weekends, maybe listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around my apartment. I have about 12 hours a week I'd dedicate to studying, but it wasn't really structured. I'd often just study when I felt like it or when something was due, which looking back wasn't the best approach.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on. I didn't have a fixed schedule where I'd sit down at the same time every day. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram more, but otherwise I'd let things slide. Some weeks I barely touched anything, other weeks I'd try to catch up on everything at once.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got a notification about something being due or when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd go on just to see if there were new discussion posts or if the professor uploaded anything. It wasn't really a daily habit for me, more like when I felt I needed to or when guilt kicked in about falling behind.
Q4: There was a brief period early in the semester where I tried to make it routine, checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. But that didn't last long. Life got in the way, my motivation dropped, and it just became something I did sporadically. The gamified tasks they introduced were interesting at first, but even that novelty wore off after a while.
Q5: The lecture recordings were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I could play them while commuting or just relaxing, and the information would sink in better than reading. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things I was confused about. Those audio-based resources really matched how my brain works.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read through them properly. The written assignments I kept putting off too, which is why my completion rate was so low. Reading dense text just doesn't work for me, and I kept telling myself I'd get to it later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to understanding the material or if it was actually engaging. The gamified tasks were interesting because they broke things into smaller challenges. But if something felt like busywork or if I couldn't see how it would help me on the exam, I'd deprioritize it heavily.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't really matter, even when they probably would have helped me. Required ones had this weight to them, but even then I'd procrastinate. I think knowing something was optional gave me permission to skip it entirely. The gamified elements tried to make optional stuff more appealing, but my low motivation made it hard to care.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying outside the LMS that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone during walks or while cooking. I also used YouTube videos to understand concepts better when the course materials weren't clicking. My notes from listening were all in a physical notebook that nobody would ever see.
Q10: There were definitely times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd replay a downloaded lecture multiple times to really absorb it, or when I'd discuss concepts with a friend over coffee. I also spent time just thinking through problems in my head during commutes. None of that leaves any digital trace.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd access a lecture recording once on the LMS, download it, and then listen to it maybe three or four more times offline. Same with some of the audio explanations the professor posted. The LMS would only show that one initial access, but I was actually engaging with that content repeatedly on my own terms.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard for me emotionally. I was feeling pretty sad and disconnected from everything, not just this course. My attendance dropped, I stopped caring as much, and logging into the LMS felt like a chore. The anxiety about falling behind made it even harder to actually do anything about it, which is a frustrating cycle.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected me. I wasn't in any extracurriculars, but I still had other courses and personal stuff going on. When I was tired or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections entirely rather than seeking help.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd log in, scroll through some materials, maybe watch part of a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt pointless afterward.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. The ones I did submit, I actually did well on, but getting started was the hardest part. I think my low motivation and the sadness I was feeling made everything seem overwhelming. It was easier to avoid things and deal with the consequences later than to face them head-on.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore everything until something was due soon, then scramble to complete it. The LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It's not a healthy pattern, but without that external pressure, I just couldn't make myself engage.
Q17: When I actually submitted work and got feedback, it did motivate me a little to keep going. But since I completed so few assignments, I didn't get much feedback to work with. The lack of regular feedback made it feel like my efforts didn't matter much, which fed into my low motivation even more.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard, but it didn't really accommodate different learning styles well. There was a lot of reading-heavy content that didn't work for me as an auditory learner. The gamified tasks were a nice touch and broke the monotony, but they weren't enough to overcome my overall disengagement.
Q19: Having lecture recordings available made engagement easier for me specifically. But the heavy reliance on written materials and the overwhelming amount of content made it harder. If there had been more audio-based options or interactive discussions, I might have stayed more connected. The structure didn't play to my strengths.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce work. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading discussions, genuinely trying to understand, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat, though I didn't perform well on the exam. But my assignment scores were high when I actually submitted work. Someone could be studying effectively offline, using external resources, and only touching the LMS minimally. The learning happens elsewhere, but the results still show up on assessments.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. They also miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the mental processing that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, real engagement is when I'm actively listening, making connections, and thinking about how concepts apply. It's a mental state, not just a behavior.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally this semester or how that affected everything. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or the conversations I had trying to understand concepts. It also wouldn't show that when I did engage properly, I actually understood the material well. My low exam score doesn't reflect what I'm capable of when I'm in a better headspace.
